sound_type: low chipping calls near cliff colony -- series of low, soft chip notes at 3000-5000 Hz heard near waterfall nesting sites; quieter and lower-pitched than Vaux's Swift; partially masked by waterfall; E=4
ecosystem: High-elevation cliff faces behind or near waterfalls. Most unusual nesting habitat of any NA bird. Forages at high altitude (1000-3000m). Last NA bird to have nest discovered (1901). Disappears to high altitude during day. Largest NA swift.
season: Summer breeder in PNW. Returns to nesting sites May-June. Departs August-September. Winters in western Brazil/Bolivia (confirmed 2010s via satellite tracking).
family: Apodidae (2nd entry. Swift contrast pair with Vaux's Swift at 147.)

coordinates.description: Black Swifts nest at scattered waterfall sites in the Cascade Range and other western mountains. Known sites in North Cascades and Olympic Mountains.

taxonomy_note.description: First Black Swift entry. SECOND APODIDAE. Swift contrast pair with Vaux's Swift (147). First genus: Cypseloides. Last NA bird to have nest discovered (1901). Waterfall nesting. Largest NA swift. Daily high-altitude disappearance. Migration to Brazil confirmed 2010s.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Cypseloides from Greek 'kypselos' (swift, hollow vessel) + '-oides' (resembling) -- swift-like. niger from Latin 'black' -- referring to the dark plumage.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Cypseloides -- first entry. The 'cloud swifts' -- high-altitude, waterfall-nesting.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Second Apodidae. Swift contrast pair: Vaux's (communal chimney, E=4) vs. Black (solitary waterfall, E=4).

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Chip: 3000-5000 Hz. Lower than Vaux's Swift (5000-8000).
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Chipping: 3000-5000 Hz. Low for a swift. Soft. Partially masked by waterfall.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual chip: 0.03-0.06 seconds. Colony calling at dawn departure and dusk return only.
song_metrics.note_structure: LOW CHIPPING: Series of soft chip notes. Lower-pitched and softer than Vaux's. Partially masked by waterfall white noise.
song_metrics.note_types: Chip (primary, colony communication), trill (excitement near colony), screech (alarm, very rare).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: No distinct phrases. Chipping during colony arrivals/departures. Silent during high-altitude foraging.
song_metrics.timbre: Low, soft, somewhat hollow. Contrasts with the high, thin quality of Vaux's Swift. Heard within waterfall soundscape.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Individual: quiet (30-80m in still conditions). Against waterfall: audible 10-30m only. Environmental amplification by cliff face. Waterfall masking reduces effective range.
song_metrics.tempo: 3-6 chips per second. Slower than Vaux's Swift (5-10/sec).
song_metrics.context: Colony communication during dawn departure and dusk return. Silent during high-altitude daytime foraging. Brief calling windows.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. Low chipping consistent across range.
song_metrics.key_property: LOW CHIPPING AT CLIFF COLONY -- 3000-5000 Hz, partially masked by waterfall, heard during brief dawn/dusk colony visits. E=4. SECOND APODIDAE. Waterfall nesting. Last NA nest discovered (1901). Daily disappearance to high altitude. Largest NA swift. Winters Brazil. Environmental acoustics.

plumage.overall: Entirely dark blackish-brown. The darkest swift in North America. Slightly paler on forehead. No distinct markings. Large for a swift.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Large size (largest NA swift). Entirely dark. Long, broad wings. Forked tail. Near waterfall habitat diagnostic if seen at colony.
plumage.flight_pattern: Powerful, direct. Longer glides than Vaux's Swift. Less flickering, more deliberate. Capable of sustained high-altitude soaring.

physical_description.overall: Length: 18-20 cm. Weight: 40-55 g. Wingspan: 38-44 cm. Largest swift in North America. Significantly larger than Vaux's Swift (11-12 cm, 17-20g). 2-3x the mass.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Large dark swift near waterfalls or at high altitude. No similar species in PNW. Size alone separates from Vaux's.
physical_description.field_id: Look for a large, entirely dark swift near mountain waterfalls at dawn or dusk. The species is rarely seen at close range. At known colony sites, watch for birds entering/exiting the waterfall spray zone. In flight: larger, more powerful, less flickering than Vaux's Swift.

nodes.title: Waterfall Nesting -- Unique Habitat
nodes.description: Cliff ledges behind or adjacent to waterfalls. Moist, inaccessible, concealed. Shallow cup of moss/fern/mud. Most unusual nesting habitat of any NA bird. Waterfall provides protection and moisture.

nodes.title: Last Nest Discovered -- 1901
nodes.description: Last NA bird to have nest found. A.G. Prill, 1901, Colorado. Hidden by waterfall, elevation, invisible minimal nest. Searched for decades. New colonies still discovered today.

nodes.title: The Disappearing Act
nodes.description: Departs colony dawn, disappears to high altitude (1000-3000m), returns dusk. Hours unobserved. Satellite tracking: hundreds of km daily. Winters Brazil/Bolivia (confirmed 2010s). A ghost in the sky.

nodes.title: Swift Contrast Pair (147-148)
nodes.description: Vaux's (communal chimney, 35K birds, public spectacle, E=4) vs. Black (solitary waterfall, 5-20 nests, hidden mystery, E=4). Same family, same energy, opposite architecture. Public vs. concealed.

cross_references.relationship: SWIFT CONTRAST! Vaux's (communal chimney, public spectacle, 35K birds) vs. Black (solitary waterfall, hidden mystery, 5-20 nests). Same family, same E=4, opposite ecology.
HIDDEN SPECIALISTS! Both masters of concealment. Poorwill invisible on ground (camouflage + torpor). Black Swift hidden behind waterfall (concealment + altitude). Different strategies, same theme.
AERIAL CONTRAST! Nighthawk (visible, urban, crepuscular, booming) vs. Black Swift (invisible, wilderness, altitude, silent). Seen vs. unseen aerial insectivores.
